skill.name: playwright-browser-automation
skill.description: Run custom Playwright browser automation for web testing. It detects local dev servers, creates temporary test scripts, launches visible browsers, captures screenshots, and checks forms, links, layouts, and login flows.
skill.summary: Automates browser-based web tests with Playwright and temporary scripts.
skill.author: Claude Assistant

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many hits because this skill intentionally runs Playwright automation code and includes documentation examples. Review confirmed unrestricted local JavaScript execution, disabled Chromium sandbox flags, and package-manager execution as real risks. No evidence found of prompt injection or confirmed malicious exfiltration, so this is high risk rather than blocked critical.

high_findings.title: Unrestricted Local JavaScript Execution
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: run.js accepts code from a file, inline argument, or stdin, wraps it, writes it to a temporary file, and loads it with require(). This is intended for automation, but it gives generated scripts full local Node.js privileges without sandboxing.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The execution path is explicit and direct. The code is not sanitized or sandboxed before require() runs it in the local Node.js process.

high_findings.title: Chromium Sandbox Disabled by Default
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: helper launch options include --no-sandbox and --disable-setuid-sandbox. This may be needed in some containers, but it weakens isolation when visiting untrusted pages.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flags are hardcoded in the default launch options. The impact depends on the pages visited, but the weakened browser isolation is real.

medium_findings.title: Automatic Package Manager Execution
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: the executor runs npm install and npx playwright install chromium when Playwright is missing. The commands are hardcoded, so command injection is unlikely, but first-run package execution expands supply-chain risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The child_process usage is confirmed and hardcoded. The risk is from package-manager execution, not from attacker-controlled shell arguments.

medium_findings.title: Broad Local File Read and Temporary File Write
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: run.js reads any existing path supplied as its first argument and writes generated execution files into the skill directory. This is expected behavior, but it increases impact if untrusted paths or code are provided.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are direct and synchronous. They are part of the intended executor design, but no path allowlist is enforced.

medium_findings.title: Prisma Credential and Screenshot Schema Bundled With Skill
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: prisma/schema.prisma defines database, credential, webhook, and screenshot storage models. No evidence found that this schema is executed by the Playwright wrapper, but it is unrelated sensitive backend surface in the package.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive model definitions are present, but search found no reference from the executor or helper files. This appears to be unused bundled material rather than active exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Playwright $$eval Misidentified as eval()
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: lib/helpers.js uses Playwright $$eval to extract element text from the page. This is not string-based JavaScript eval in the local Node.js process.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged token is a Playwright API method call with a function callback. It does not evaluate attacker-provided source text locally.

low_findings.title: Localhost Dev Server Probing Is Expected
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: helper network access sends HEAD requests only to localhost ports to detect running dev servers. This matches the skill purpose and does not show external exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hostname is fixed to localhost and the method is HEAD. The behavior is disclosed in SKILL.md and supports browser testing workflows.

low_findings.title: Documentation Examples Trigger Many Static Hits
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: many SKILL.md findings are example commands, localhost URLs, /tmp paths, and Playwright snippets. They document expected use rather than hidden executable logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The content is in markdown examples and usage instructions. It is still relevant because users may follow it, but it is not hidden code execution by itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: Generated Code Loaded With require()
dangerous_patterns.description: run.js writes assembled automation code to a .temp-execution file and immediately loads it with require(). This pattern executes generated JavaScript with local process privileges.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write and require calls are adjacent in the executor. This is a direct generated-code execution pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Inline Code Interpolation Into Async Wrapper
dangerous_patterns.description: run.js inserts provided code into an async wrapper template. This is powerful automation behavior, but it provides no isolation boundary for untrusted code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The provided code is interpolated directly into executable JavaScript. The wrapper adds error handling, not sandboxing.

dangerous_patterns.title: Disabled Browser Sandbox Flags
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper default browser launch arguments disable Chromium sandbox protections. This should be opt-in or documented as a container compatibility tradeoff.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The flags are visible in default options and apply unless caller overrides launch arguments. This is a concrete risky default.

content.value_statement: Manual browser checks are slow and easy to miss. This skill creates and runs Playwright automation for pages, forms, screenshots, links, and responsive layouts.
actual_capabilities: Detects common localhost development servers before testing.
Runs custom Playwright scripts through a local Node.js executor.
Launches Chromium, Firefox, or WebKit through Playwright helpers.
Captures screenshots and checks responsive layouts across viewports.
Automates forms, login flows, clicks, typing, and table extraction.
Provides helper functions for retries, cookie banners, and page readiness.
limitations: Requires Node.js, npm, Playwright, and browser dependencies on the local machine.
Executes generated JavaScript with local process privileges.
May need a graphical display when using the default visible browser mode.
Does not provide a sandbox for untrusted automation scripts or websites.

use_cases.title: Check a Local Web App
use_cases.description: Detect a running dev server, open the app, capture screenshots, and verify core page behavior.

use_cases.title: Validate Release Flows
use_cases.description: Run browser checks for login, navigation, forms, links, and responsive layouts before a release.

use_cases.title: Review UI Changes
use_cases.description: Use visible browser automation to inspect key screens and collect screenshots for review.

prompt_templates.title: Open a Page
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check for a local page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Playwright to find my local dev server, open the main page, and report the title and visible errors.

prompt_templates.title: Capture Responsive Screenshots
prompt_templates.prompt: Test my local app at desktop, tablet, and mobile sizes. Save screenshots and summarize layout problems.

prompt_templates.title: Test a Form Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Validate user input and submission behavior
prompt_templates.prompt: Automate the contact form, fill realistic test values, submit it, and confirm the success state appears.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Critical Journey
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Playwright script for the login-to-dashboard journey, include waits, screenshots, error handling, and a concise result summary.

output_examples.input: Check the marketing page on my local dev server.
output_examples.output: The skill detects the local server, opens the page, captures desktop and mobile screenshots, and reports visible layout or console issues.
The skill fills the login form, submits it, waits for the dashboard route, and reports whether the redirect succeeded.

output_examples.input: Find broken external links on the homepage.
output_examples.output: The skill opens the homepage, checks link responses, and returns a short list of failing links with status details.

best_practices: Review generated Playwright code before execution when pages or inputs are sensitive.
Use localhost or trusted test environments for automation runs.
Store screenshots and test artifacts in temporary paths with clear names.
anti_patterns: Do not run unreviewed inline JavaScript from untrusted prompts.
Do not use real production credentials in generated test scripts.
Do not browse hostile websites with the browser sandbox disabled.

faq.question: What does this skill automate?
faq.answer: It automates browser actions with Playwright, including navigation, screenshots, forms, links, login flows, and responsive checks.

faq.question: Does it need Playwright installed?
faq.answer: Yes. The executor checks for Playwright and can run setup commands when dependencies are missing.

faq.question: Can it test localhost apps?
faq.answer: Yes. It probes common localhost ports and asks for a choice when multiple dev servers are found.

faq.question: Can it run in headless mode?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples prefer visible browsers, but scripts can request headless mode when needed.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive credentials?
faq.answer: Use caution. Generated scripts run locally with full Node.js privileges, so avoid real production secrets.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk high?
faq.answer: The skill intentionally executes generated JavaScript locally and disables browser sandbox flags by default.
